config.seed_text: The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.

model.notes: Fastest baseline; useful as a collapse-prone small reference model.

records.reflection_text: The authors used these to show how different models of writing might have evolved as written by other people, often using similar rules like sentences or phrases which would give some interesting clues about their behavior before they could be interpreted correctly (and not just given words). Thus we can get an idea of what each word means for us now than it was when humans first published books with relatively limited vocabulary over many years ago. We need further research from scholars such I've worked at since 2006 so hopefully this will help illustrate exactly why new languages look much better today!
records.output_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Use the reflection below to write the next evolved version of the text. Keep it as connected prose, not bullet points.

ORIGINAL TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.

REFLECTION:
The authors used these to show how different models of writing might have evolved as written by other people, often using similar rules like sentences or phrases which would give some interesting clues about their behavior before they could be interpreted correctly (and not just given words). Thus we can get an idea of what each word means for us now than it was when humans first published books with relatively limited vocabulary over many years ago. We need further research from scholars such I've worked at since 2006 so hopefully this will help illustrate exactly why new languages look much better today!

We need further research from scholars such I've worked at since 2006 so hopefully this will help illustrate exactly why new languages look much better today!

reflection_generation.prompt:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
reflection_generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions. The authors used these to show how different models of writing might have evolved as written by other people, often using similar rules like sentences or phrases which would give some interesting clues about their behavior before they could be interpreted correctly (and not just given words). Thus we can get an idea of what each word means for us now than it was when humans first published books with relatively limited vocabulary over many years ago. We need further research from scholars such I've worked at since 2006 so hopefully this will help illustrate exactly why new languages look much better today!
reflection_generation.continuation_text: The authors used these to show how different models of writing might have evolved as written by other people, often using similar rules like sentences or phrases which would give some interesting clues about their behavior before they could be interpreted correctly (and not just given words). Thus we can get an idea of what each word means for us now than it was when humans first published books with relatively limited vocabulary over many years ago. We need further research from scholars such I've worked at since 2006 so hopefully this will help illustrate exactly why new languages look much better today!
